name: Nintendo Wii
short_description: A home game console whose motion-sensing Wii Remote made physical gesture the primary input, drawing families and non-gamers into shared play.
long_description: Released in November 2006, the Nintendo Wii deliberately broke from the console industry's race for raw processing power and instead invested in a novel physical interface: a handheld Wii Remote that detected three-axis acceleration and pointed at the screen via infrared sensors. The bundled Wii Sports — bowling, tennis, golf, baseball, boxing — made the interaction immediately legible to anyone who had swung an arm, and the console became a landmark multi-user device: up to four players with simultaneous remotes, full-household generational range, and a living-room social dynamic that had more in common with board game night than solo gaming. Selling 101.63 million units, the Wii demonstrated that changing the physical form of the input — from button matrix to embodied gesture — could redefine who a device is for and how many people participate at once.
ai_note: The Wii Remote uses accelerometers and infrared point-tracking for gesture input; no machine-learning or AI runs onboard — game responses are rule-based physics simulations.

images.caption: Nintendo Wii console with Wii Remote and Nunchuk controller.
images.alt_text: A white Nintendo Wii console standing vertically next to a white Wii Remote controller and its Nunchuk accessory, all against a plain white background.
